(1,9), (1,18), (2,1), (2,2), (2,3), (2,4), (2,6), (2,12), (2,14), (2,16), (3,5), (3,9), (3,13), (3,14), (4,4), (4,8), (4,13), (5,3), (5,5), (5,10), (6,3), (6,5), (6,6), (6,7), (6,8), (6,10), (6,14), (6,15), (7,4), (7,13), (7,17), (8,6), (8,7), (8,10), (9,6), (9,7), (9,12), (10,1), (10,8), (10,9), (10,17), (11,2), (11,9), (11,10), (11,13), (11,16), (12,3), (12,9), (12,10), (12,11), (12,12), (12,15), (12,17), (14,8), (14,14), (14,18), (15,4), (15,9), (16,4), (16,13), (17,3), (17,7), (17,9), (17,11), (17,12), (18,5), (18,8), (18,12), (18,17)

**Reasoning for next action:**
With a large number of candidates, I need to move in a direction that maximizes the differentiation of subsequent observations. Moving North will lead to a wide variety of items (none, plant, trashcan, and desk) across these coordinates, which will help eliminate a significant portion of the candidates. For example, if I move North and observe a "plant", candidates like (1,9) (which leads to a trashcan) and (2,1) (which leads to a desk) will be eliminated.

[ACTION: move-N]

Moving East will allow me to immediately determine if I am at (10,10). If the result is "none", I will be left with two candidates, (3,15) and (8,8). Other directions (North, West, South) result in identical items across these candidates, providing no differentiation.
